context: there's a problem in the day of Hosea, in chapter 10 of Hosea, the Bible says they had a divided heart.
point_summary: Hosea 10 is cited as a warning that a divided heart—serving God without wholehearted love as the motive—is a danger the church must guard against.

context: Hosea chapter number 10, notice what the Bible says here in verse number 12. Hosea 10 and verse number 12, the Bible says this, sow to yourselves in righteousness, reap in mercy.
point_summary: Spiritual readiness for revival requires deliberate cultivation — breaking up hardened, fallow ground — so that seeking the Lord yields a harvest of righteousness rather than the iniquity reaped from unprepared hearts.

context: break up your fallow ground for it is time to seek the Lord till he come and rain righteousness upon you.
point_summary: Hosea 10:12's call to "break up your fallow ground" is applied as a summons to spiritual cultivation—agreeing with God's authority as the necessary preparation for revival.

context: In verse 13, you have plowed wickedness, you have reaped iniquity. You have eaten the fruit of lies because thou didst trust in thy way in the multitude of thy mighty men.
point_summary: Hosea 10:13 is read as the negative counterpart to verse 12—Israel's plowing of wickedness and reaping of iniquity illustrating the harvest principle that makes breaking up fallow ground and agreeing with God urgently necessary.

context: Therefore shall a tumult arise among thy people and all thy fortresses shall be spoiled as Shalman spoiled Beth Arbel in the day of battle. The mother was dashed in pieces upon her children.
point_summary: Hosea 10:14 is cited as contextual backdrop to verse 12's call to "break up your fallow ground," illustrating the consequence of spiritual wickedness and misplaced trust that revival-readiness must reverse.

context: And the question specifically is from the second chapter, a reference in verse number 15 specifically.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Bible says in Hosea 1, in verse number 1,
point_summary: Hosea 1:1 establishes the chronological framework of Hosea's ministry, anchoring it to the reigns of specific kings of Judah and Israel, even though his prophetic calling was directed primarily to the northern ten tribes.

context: the word of the Lord that came unto Hosea, the son of Berei, in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah.
point_summary: Hosea 1:1 is used to establish the precise historical and royal chronology of Hosea's ministry, anchoring his prophecy to named kings of both Judah and Israel, specifically identifying Jeroboam II as the northern king during this period.

context: The beginning of the word of the Lord by Hosea. Notice the Bible says, And the Lord said to Hosea, Go take unto thee a wife of whoredoms and children of whoredoms.
point_summary: God's command to Hosea to marry a wife of whoredoms served as an enacted illustration of Israel's spiritual adultery—her departure from the Lord—establishing the indictment that frames the entire book.

context: Here's the question that was posed in Hosea chapter 2. The question reads this way, what is the meaning of the valley of Achor for a door of hope in Hosea 2 and verse number 15?
point_summary: Despite Israel's guilt warranting judgment, God promises to transform the valley of Achor—a place of past consequence—into a door of hope, demonstrating his long-suffering, mercy, and love toward his wayward people.

context: therefore behold, I will allure her and bring her into the wilderness and speak comfortably unto her.
point_summary: Despite Israel's guilt and deserved judgment, God's long-suffering mercy moves Him to allure His wayward people back into the wilderness for restoration rather than abandonment.

context: them to lie down safely.
point_summary: The repeated phrase "that day" appearing in verses 16, 18, and 21 signals a specific, divinely appointed future moment that structures the entire promise of Hosea 2.

context: I will betroth thee unto me forever, yea, I will betroth thee unto me in righteousness, and in judgment, and in lovingkindness, and in mercies.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I will even betroth thee unto me in faithfulness, and thou shalt know the Lord.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And it shall come to pass in that day, I will hear, saith the Lord, I will hear the heavens, and they shall hear the earth.
point_summary: The repeated phrase "that day" appearing in verses 16, 18, and 21 signals a unified eschatological promise toward which the entire passage points.

context: And the earth shall hear the corn, and the wine, and the oil, and they shall hear Jezreel.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And I will sow her unto me in the earth, and I will have mercy upon her that had not obtained mercy. And I will say to them which were not my people, thou art my people, and they shall say, Thou art my God.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: it shall be at that day
point_summary: The repeated phrase "that day" in verses 16, 18, and 21 marks God's promised restoration as a distinct, future eschatological event anchoring the entire passage's hope.

context: and in that day
point_summary: The repeated phrase "that day" in verses 16, 18, and 21 marks God's promised future restoration as a distinct, divinely appointed era of covenant renewal and peace.

context: and it shall come to pass in that day
point_summary: The repeated phrase "that day" in verses 16, 18, and 21 marks a unified eschatological promise, signaling that God's restoration of Israel belongs to a single, coming day of fulfillment.

context: removing idolatry
point_summary: God's promised restoration of Israel includes the complete removal of idolatry — the names of the Baals erased from her mouth and memory entirely.

context: the Bible says in all of this indictment and all of this wickedness and the spiritual adultery, the judgment that would come, God says the Valley of Achor for a door of hope
point_summary: God's transformation of the Valley of Achor — a site of certain judgment — into a door of hope signals that biblical hope is a confident expectation, not mere wishful thinking.

context: ...get back to Hosea chapter number two...
point_summary: In Hosea 2:15, God transforms the Valley of Achor—Israel's historic site of judgment—into a door of hope, where "hope" carries the biblical meaning of confident expectation.

context: it talks about the covenant that he's gonna make with the beast of the field and all of that in verse 18
point_summary: In the millennial reign, God will restore creation to its pre-flood harmony by making a covenant with the beasts of the field, the fowls, and creeping things, reversing the effects of judgment.

context: In verse 19 and 20, he talks about that betrothal and that relationship
point_summary: God's betrothal promise in verse 19 is linked to the millennial restoration, fulfilling the relational shift from "Baali" to "Ishi" hinted at in verse 16—a covenant not yet realized but certain to come.

context: In verse 19 and 20, he talks about that betrothal and that relationship
point_summary: Hosea 2:20's betrothal language is connected to verse 16's shift from "Baali" to "Ishi," together forming God's unfulfilled but certain promise of restored covenant relationship with Israel.

context: he gave hint to in verse number 16 with regard to the names that not Bali, but Ishi
point_summary: Verse 16's shift from "Baali" (my master) to "Ishi" (my husband) anticipates the restored covenant intimacy God will establish with Israel during the millennial reign, previewing the betrothal language of verses 19–20.

context: the Valley of Achor, a place known, it's a place of judgment, trouble, disturbance will be a door of hope
point_summary: The Valley of Achor, a place of judgment and trouble, is promised by God to become a door of hope—a promise not yet fulfilled but awaiting realization at the millennial reign.

context: when Israel was a child, the Bible says, then I loved him and called my son out of Egypt.
point_summary: Hosea 11:1 is cited to show that behind God's mighty deliverance of Israel from Egypt lay divine love as the motivating force, not power alone.

context: 'Sow to yourselves in righteousness, 'reap in mercy, break up your fallow ground, 'for it is time to seek the Lord, 'till He come and reign righteousness upon you.'
point_summary: Hosea 10:12's call — "it is time to seek the Lord" — frames revival as God's sovereign work for which His people must nonetheless actively prepare themselves.

context: The heart, plow, it's time to plow. Break up your fallow ground.
point_summary: Genuine revival requires not merely incremental moral improvement but a thoroughly broken and contrite heart, as Hosea's call to "break up your fallow ground" demands complete cleansing, not just being cleaner.

context: agricultural analogy that's being used here in this passage of Scripture in Hosea 10
point_summary: Hosea 10's agricultural imagery of breaking up fallow ground is applied as a two-stage pathway to revival: the heart must first be plowed in brokenness over offending God, then immediately planted.

context: sow to yourselves in righteousness, reap in mercy
point_summary: Revival's pathway requires intentional sowing of righteousness—not merely breaking up fallow ground, but planting God's Word—because the law of sowing and reaping guarantees that righteous seed yields a harvest of mercy.

context: And so we find here that it's time to plow back to Hosea 10, it's time to plant. Let me say this, it's time to pursue. It says this in verse number 12, for it is time to seek the Lord till he come and reign righteousness upon you.
point_summary: Revival requires laser-focused pursuit of God above all competing temporal distractions, grounded in Hosea's command that "it is time to seek the Lord."

context: In Hosea 10 verses 12 and 13, there's a great contrast between those two verses of scripture.
point_summary: Hosea 10:12 presents a different path than the one described in verse 13, calling God's people to a way of seeking God himself rather than merely filling churches with men.

context: In Hosea 10 verses 12 and 13, there's a great contrast between those two verses of scripture.
point_summary: Hosea 10:13 describes the failed path Israel had been traveling — the path of self-reliance and wrong pursuit — set in deliberate contrast to the God-seeking way called for in verse 12.

context: Ephraim, he hath mixed himself among the people.
point_summary: Revival begins not with critiquing the world but with self-examination, and Hosea 7:8's image of Ephraim—unaware that strangers have devoured his strength—frames that call to let God examine the believer personally.

context: is a cake not turned. Strangers have devoured his strength, and he knoweth it not.
point_summary: Israel's spiritual compromise — mixing with the nations, unaware of its own decay — is presented as a warning the preacher applies equally to himself and his congregation.

context: Yea, gray hairs are here and there upon him, yet he knoweth not.
point_summary: Spiritual decline can go unnoticed by the one experiencing it, as Ephraim's gray hairs and devoured strength illustrate a people unaware of their own deterioration.

context: And the pride of Israel testifieth to his face, and they do not return to the Lord their God, nor seek him for all this.
point_summary: Israel's pride is indicted as a witness against herself, testifying to her face yet leaving her unwilling to return to or seek the Lord.

context: Ephraim also is like a silly dove without heart. They call to Egypt, they go to Assyria.
point_summary: Israel's foolish instability — seeking help from Egypt and Assyria rather than God — illustrates the danger of turning to human alliances instead of returning to the Lord who redeemed them.

context: When they shall go, I will spread my net upon them. I will bring them down as the fowls of the heaven. I will chastise them as their congregation hath heard.
point_summary: God's judgment falls on Ephraim like a net because, despite redemption, the people fled from him, spoke lies against him, and never cried to him with their heart.

context: Woe unto them, for they have fled from me. Destruction unto them, because they have transgressed against me, though I have redeemed them, yet they have spoken lies against me.
point_summary: God's indictment of Israel in Hosea 7:13 centers on the contradiction of a redeemed people who flee from, transgress against, and speak lies against the very God who delivered them.

context: And they have not cried unto me with their heart. When they howl upon their beds, they assemble themselves for corn and wine, and they rebel against me.
point_summary: Outward religious howling without genuine heart-cry constitutes rebellion against God, not true prayer — the distinction Hosea 7:14 draws between Israel's self-serving lament and authentic seeking of the Lord.

context: strangers have devoured his strength, and he knoweth it not, yea, gray hairs are here and there upon him, yet he knoweth not.
point_summary: Hosea 7:9's "gray hairs" signals not wisdom gained but decline unnoticed—Israel's strength was being consumed by strangers while she remained oblivious to her own spiritual deterioration.

context: Ephraim, he hath mixed himself among the people. Ephraim is a cake not turned.
point_summary: Hosea 7:8's image of a half-baked cake illustrates the first of four "spiritual gray hairs"—a false testimony produced when God's people blend so thoroughly with surrounding culture that their distinctiveness is lost.

context: break up your fallow ground
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I will go and return to my place till they acknowledge their offense and seek my face. And in their affliction they will seek me early.
point_summary: God's withdrawal to "his place" until his people acknowledge their offense is presented as a gracious design, with affliction serving as the instrument that drives them to seek him early.

context: Come and let us return unto the Lord. For he hath torn and he will heal us. And he hath smitten and he will bind us up.
point_summary: Hosea 6:1 is cited to show that divine chastening—tearing and smiting—is purposefully restorative, designed to draw God's people back into fellowship with him.

context: Therefore, turn thou to thy God. Keep mercy and judgment and wait on thy God continually.
point_summary: Hosea 12:6 is presented as the direct remedy for Israel's turmoil and desolation: turn to God, keep mercy and judgment, and wait on Him continually.

context: O Israel, return unto the Lord thy God, for thou hast fallen by thine iniquity.
point_summary: Hosea 14:1's call—"return unto the Lord thy God, for thou hast fallen by thine iniquity"—is cited as a prophetic echo of Lamentations' plea, underscoring that iniquity causes the drift requiring divine restoration.

context: and now they sin, what does it say? More and more. And now they sin more and more and have made them molten images of their silver and idols according to their own understanding
point_summary: Israel's persistent, escalating idolatry—sinning "more and more" and fashioning molten images—is presented as the historical pattern that Psalm 4's warning, "stand in awe and sin not," was meant to arrest.

context: I will go and return to my place till they acknowledge their offense and seek my face in their affliction they will seek me early.
point_summary: Hosea 5:15 is cited to show that God withholds His presence until His people acknowledge their offense, linking unconfessed sin directly to broken fellowship with Him.

context: Hosea 13 how they sinned more and more
point_summary: Hosea 13 is cited as a prior example of escalating sin, contrasting with Hosea 5:15's call to acknowledgment and confession as the path back to God's presence.

context: Psalm 78 and Hosea 13, they sinned more and more
point_summary: Israel's pattern of sinning more and more (Hosea 13) is set in contrast to the call to "awake to righteousness and sin not," underscoring the danger of a low or unbiblical view of God and sin.

context: Hosea chapter number 10, the first of what we'd often refer to as being the minor prophets
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: sow to yourselves in righteousness, reap in mercy, break up your fallow ground, for it is time to seek the Lord, till He come and reign righteousness upon you
point_summary: Hosea 10:12's agricultural imperatives — sow in righteousness, break up fallow ground, seek the Lord — are introduced as a call to spiritual renewal that the sermon will develop fully at its close.

context: You have plowed wickedness. You have reaped iniquity
point_summary: Hosea 10:13 is read as a warning that trusting in one's own way and human strength produces a harvest of wickedness and lies rather than righteousness.

context: Israel is an empty vine
point_summary: Israel's outward prosperity—described as a vine bringing forth fruit—masked inward spiritual bankruptcy, as increased blessing led not to faithfulness but to multiplied idolatry and a divided heart.

context: he bringeth forth fruit unto himself. According to the multitude of his fruit, he hath increased the altars. According to the goodness of his land, they have made goodly images
point_summary: Israel's indictment in Hosea 10:1 is not an absence of fruit but the misdirection of it—abundance multiplied idolatrous altars rather than devotion to God, exposing a heart divided between God and self.

context: their heart is divided
point_summary: Israel's divided heart — directing its fruit toward self and idolatrous altars rather than God — stands as the core indictment, illustrating that unfaithfulness is a matter of allegiance, not merely outward action.

context: these words as it's mentioned in verse number four that are swearing falsely in making a covenant
point_summary: False words and broken covenants bring judgment, illustrated in Hosea 10:4 as the reason divine punishment springs up like hemlock in the furrows of the field.

context: the inhabitants of Samaria shall fear because of the calves of Beth Avon for the people thereof shall mourn over it
point_summary: Israel's divided heart, expressed in false worship at Samaria's calf-shrines, brings mourning as God's glory departs and judgment falls.

context: it also shall be, it shall be also carried into Assyria for a present to King Jerob
point_summary: Israel's divided heart, false covenants, and idolatry culminate in national shame and exile to Assyria — judgment that springs directly from unfaithfulness to God.

context: Notice verse nine, the Bible says this, O Israel, thou hast sinned and then notice this phrase from the days of Gibeah
point_summary: Israel's persistent sin is traced back to "the days of Gibeah," connecting the nation's present guilt under Hosea to a deep-rooted pattern of iniquity stretching back to the period of the judges.

context: he says to them here in Hosea 10 in verse number 9, O Israel thou hast sinned from the days of Gibeah
point_summary: Israel's persistent sin is traced by God all the way back to the notorious wickedness at Gibeah in Judges 19, establishing a pattern of rebellion that has continued unbroken into Hosea's day.

context: We find that also in Hosea 9 in verse number nine, there's a reference to the days of Gibeah
point_summary: Hosea 9:9's reference to "the days of Gibeah" anchors Israel's present corruption in a historical pattern of deep, remembered iniquity that God will not overlook but will visit with judgment.

context: Notice, and maybe on the same page for you in Hosea 9 in verse nine
point_summary: Hosea 9:9 is cited as a parallel indictment, linking Israel's present corruption directly to the notorious wickedness of Gibeah, with God's promised remembrance and visitation of that sin as the consequence.

context: We read of these things here in chapter number 10
point_summary: Israel's sin in Hosea 10 is not fruitlessness but self-directed fruitfulness—abundance turned toward idolatry, a divided heart, and false swearing rooted in corruption stretching back to the days of Gibeah.

context: In Hosea 4 in verse number one, hear the word of the Lord, you children of Israel
point_summary: God's indictment of Israel in Hosea 4:1 — that the land lacks truth, mercy, and knowledge of God — is presented as the loving confrontation of a God who sends his word precisely because he cares for his people.

context: hear the word of the Lord, ye children of Israel, for the Lord, notice what it says, the Lord hath a controversy with the inhabitants of the land because there is no truth nor mercy nor knowledge of God in the land
point_summary: God's indictment of Israel in Hosea 4:1 — that the land lacks truth, mercy, and knowledge of God — frames the broader controversy the Lord brings against his people for rejecting his available word.

context: My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge. But notice this next phrase, because thou hast rejected knowledge
point_summary: Destruction from lack of knowledge is not God's withholding of truth but Israel's willful rejection of available revelation, a rejection that brings reciprocal rejection from God himself.

context: I will also reject thee, that thou shalt be no priest to me, seeing thou hast forgotten the law of thy God
point_summary: Israel's destruction came not from unavailability of God's word but from the deliberate rejection of it, a choice that brought reciprocal rejection from God himself.

context: as they were increased, so they sinned against me
point_summary: Prosperity's spiritual danger is underscored here: as Israel's increase grew, so did their sin, fulfilling God's warning that abundance breeds forgetfulness of Him.

context: Verse eight, they eat up the sin of my people and they set their heart on their iniquity and there shall be like people like priests and I will punish them for their ways and reward them their doings
point_summary: Prosperity unchecked by faithfulness leads to compounding sin, and God's law of sowing and reaping ensures that corrupt priests and people alike will be punished according to their ways.

context: In verse 18, their drink is sour, they have committed whoredoms.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: notice in chapter seven, in verse number 14, notice what God says. And they have not cried unto me with their heart. When they howled upon their beds, they assembled themselves corn and wine and they rebel against me.
point_summary: Outward religious expression — howling, assembling for grain and wine — is exposed as hollow when the heart has not genuinely cried out to God, making it indistinguishable from rebellion.

context: Chapter eight and verse 14, for Israel hath forgotten his maker
point_summary: Israel's forgetting of God is shown to produce false substitutes—temple-building and fortified cities—in place of true devotion, paralleling Jeremiah's similar indictment of Judah on the eve of her own captivity.

context: Israel hath forgotten his maker, and buildeth temples, and Judah hath multiplied fenced cities, but I will send a fire upon his cities, and it shall devour the palaces thereof
point_summary: Forgetting God while multiplying religious structures and fortifications reveals not hidden sin but known rebellion, for God sees and knows every act of disobedience without being informed.

context: the indictment against Israel in Hosea chapter 10
point_summary: Hosea 10's indictment against Israel demonstrates that God requires no confession to know guilt — He sees the divided heart, the secret sin, and the outward religiosity that masks inward corruption.

context: the indictment against Israel in Hosea 10
point_summary: Hosea 10's indictment against Israel prompts the question of what specific sin—illustrated here by pornography's pervasive destruction of homes and churches—God would level against the congregation today.

context: back in our text in Hosea 10, we note the instruction
point_summary: Israel's prosperity, like the church's, can mask spiritual corruption that God sees fully and will indict, withholding blessing from unconfessed sin.

context: sow to yourselves in righteousness, reap in mercy
point_summary: Hosea 10:12's command to "break up your fallow ground" is interpreted as a call to prepare hardened, divided hearts before God's seed of righteousness can take root.

context: break up your fallow ground
point_summary: Hosea 10:12's command to "break up your fallow ground" is interpreted as a call to prepare hardened, divided hearts before God's seed of righteousness can take root.

context: their heart, according to verse number two, was divided
point_summary: A divided heart, as described in verse 2, is the condition that necessitates God's instruction in verse 12 to "break up your fallow ground" — hardness requiring preparation before any seed can take root.

context: break up your fallow ground
point_summary: Hosea 10:12's command to "break up your fallow ground" is applied as a call to deliberate heart-preparation before worship, because hardened soil cannot receive the seed of God's Word.

context: I preached last week from the book of Hosea, break up your fallow ground
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the book of Hosea
point_summary: Jacob's limp after Penuel—cited through Hosea 12's reference to this encounter—is presented as the most significant and lasting mark of his wrestling with God.

context: But I want you to notice what the Bible says in the book of Hosea. Turn to the book of Hosea if you will in chapter number 10.
point_summary: Spiritual receptivity requires deliberate preparation of the heart, illustrated by the agricultural command in Hosea 10:12 to break up fallow ground before seed can bear fruit.

context: in Hosea chapter 10 and verse number 12, the Bible says this Sow to yourselves in righteousness reap in mercy
point_summary: Hosea 10:12's call to "break up your fallow ground" frames spiritual revival as deliberate soil preparation—cultivating the heart so that seed sown in righteousness yields a harvest of mercy.

context: Verse 13, you have plowed wickedness, you have reaped iniquity
point_summary: Hosea 10:13 illustrates the inescapable law of sowing and reaping—wickedness planted yields iniquity harvested—set against verse 12's call to sow righteousness and reap mercy.
